content.problemStatement:
Given a `N x M`(N rows and M columns) matrix, print it upside down, i.e, last row should come first, second last should come second......so on..and finally first row should come in last.

See the following example:




Input Format

The first line of input will contain two space separated integers NNN and MMM, denoting the no. of rows and columns in input matrix.
Next NNN lines contains MMM space separated integers, the elements of the matrix.


Output Format

Output NNN lines contains MMM space separated integers, the elements of the given matrix in upside down manner.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two space‑separated integers N and M denoting the number of rows and columns of the matrix. This is followed by N lines each containing M space‑separated integers representing the matrix rows.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output N lines. Each line contains M space‑separated integers of the original matrix printed in upside‑down order (last row first, …, first row last). Separate the outputs of consecutive test cases by a blank line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The matrix has three rows. Reversing the row order yields the last row first, then the middle row, then the first row.
Two rows are swapped: the second row becomes first and the first becomes second.
Only one row exists, so the output is identical to the input.

solution.approach: The task is to reverse the order of rows in a given matrix. For each test case we read the matrix row by row, store it (e.g., in a 2‑D array or a vector of vectors) and then iterate over the stored rows from the last index down to zero, printing each row as space‑separated values. This directly yields the upside‑down matrix. The algorithm is straightforward and runs in linear time with respect to the number of elements.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N*M) to store the matrix (can be reduced to O(N) if rows are printed while reading, but storing is simpler and fits the constraints)
